personas.persona: Chad built the firm on repeat business and treats every outbound page as a reflection of his own name, which is why nothing leaves without his signature. His edits push toward shorter sentences, concrete verbs, and a clear ask in the first paragraph. In writing he is courtly with clients and blunt inside the firm, and he has no patience for hedging language or consultant jargon.

Jenna runs engagements day to day and writes most of what the client actually reads, drafting for Chad's signature and anticipating his red pen. Her style is structured and economical: findings first, support second, and a recommendation the client executive can act on without reading page two. Colleagues know her for tight meeting minutes and for chasing down loose ends nobody else noticed.

Jacob is the workhorse on pricing studies and integration checklists, most comfortable when the analysis is in a table and the prose just explains it. He writes plainly and a little dryly, tends toward longer, careful sentences, and flags any claim he cannot trace back to a source. Inside the firm he is the one who asks the uncomfortable question in the kickoff meeting.

Lisa is the junior on the team, sharp with spreadsheets and eager in a way that shows in her drafts, which arrive thorough but overlong until Jenna trims them. She takes meticulous notes in client meetings and is usually the one turning them into minutes the same afternoon. Her written voice is earnest and precise, still shaking off a slightly academic habit of qualifying everything.

Megan keeps the office running: scheduling, invoicing paperwork, letterhead stock, and the filing system only she fully understands. Her emails are short, friendly, and relentlessly practical, usually ending with exactly what she needs and by when. She is on a first-name basis with every client's assistant and knows which partner to nudge to get a signature out the door.
